puzzle0.title: SIGNAL TUNING
puzzle0.description: Tune the emergency frequency until the hidden survivor transmission locks.

event10.text: If you are a recording, lie to me.

event12.text: Actually don't. I am very tired of being lied to by electrical equipment.

event14.text: Just send something back. Anything. My relay is drifting and I can't keep shouting into static.

event1.text: I will. But your signal is tearing apart.

event3.text: If you want my name, keep the channel alive long enough for me to say it.

event5.text: And if this is some rescue protocol, congratulations. You have terrible timing.

event7.text: Which is still better than no timing.

event5.text: Fine. Verify me. I would verify me too.

event7.text: I've been alone long enough to stop trusting my own echo.

event0.title: EMERGENCY AUDIO TRANSCRIPT
event0.content:
CHANNEL: 7.14 MHz
SIGNAL STATE: DISTORTED
VOICEPRINT: UNKNOWN FEMALE
RECOVERED FRAGMENTS:

"...anyone..."
"...relay is drifting..."
"...not Hale..."
"...lower access spine..."

Recommended action: manual signal tuning required.
event0.caption: Text reconstruction of distorted emergency audio from channel 7.14.

event4.text: The carrier keeps drifting like the relay has a death wish.

event6.text: Which, honestly, would fit the general personality of this station.

event8.text: If you can tune it, do it now. If you wait too long, I go back to being a rumor in the static.

event1.text: Do not let that channel lead.

event3.text: Tune it if you have to, but keep one hand on the exit.

event5.text: And before you ask, yes, I hear how useless that advice is from inside a terminal.

event7.text: Still. One hand on the exit.

event9.text: Also... if she gives you a name, don't assume a name means safety.

event11.text: Names are easy. Survival is harder.

event5.text: I had you for a second. Don't make me go back to static.

event7.text: Hold it steady when it bites. The drift gets worse right before the lock.

event1.text: Listen. Not to the words. To the pull.

event3.text: It drifts below seven, then snaps back. It wants you to overcorrect.

event5.text: Don't chase it. Let it come to 7.14 and hold there.

event7.text: I hate that I sound like Hale right now.

choice1.text: ASK HALE FOR A STABILIZATION TIP.

event3.text: Don't chase the carrier. Predict the drift. If the frequency is fighting you, it is probably trying to make you move too much.

event5.text: Small corrections. Hold on the target. Let the lock build.

event7.text: And don't tell her I helped. She will be insufferable about it.
I forgot how to talk to people without wondering if they were already dead.

event14.text: I was starting to think I'd die talking to static.

event16.text: I spent three days talking to a maintenance drone once.

event20.text: Still a better listener than Patel.

event23.text: That was a joke. I think.

event27.text: I'm pinned near a damaged relay console in the lower access spine. Oxygen is low. I need to move before this place becomes my coffin.

event29.text: And if Ethan Hale is on your other channel, you need to stop letting him steer you.

event0.title: SURVIVOR BIOMETRIC SCAN
event0.content:
CHANNEL SOURCE: 7.14 MHz
VOICEPRINT MATCH: MAYA CHEN
LIFE SIGNS: ACTIVE
OXYGEN: 18%
LOCATION ESTIMATE: DAMAGED RELAY CONSOLE / LOWER ACCESS SPINE
VISUAL FEED: UNAVAILABLE
STATUS: ALIVE / CRITICAL
event0.caption: Automated survivor verification generated from channel telemetry. No image recovered.

event9.text: Oxygen is at eighteen percent, which sounds better than it feels.

event11.text: The relay console is cracked open. I can use it as an anchor point, or I can leave now and try the access spine.

event13.text: If I stay, I might pull more data. If I move, I might keep breathing. I hate that those are different options.

choice1.text: MOVE MAYA TOWARD THE ACCESS SPINE.

event3.text: The cracked relay console. I named it Malcolm.

event5.text: Malcolm and I are not friends, but Malcolm is keeping me alive, so we're being professional.

event7.title: MAYA RELAY TELEMETRY
event7.content:
LOWER ACCESS SPINE
OXYGEN BLEED: ACTIVE
AIRFLOW SOURCE: SERVICE LOCK C
SECONDARY MOVEMENT TRACE: DETECTED NEAR DOOR 12 RETURN LINE
NOTE: trace does not match Maya's current position.
event7.caption: Maya extracted this while stabilizing the damaged relay console.

event9.text: I got something. Movement trace near the Door 12 return line.

event15.text: Bad news: something else is moving.

event3.text: Good. I was one bad thought away from arguing with Malcolm again.

event8.text: Narrow. Cold. Smells like hot plastic and old blood.

event10.text: I don't have the telemetry from the console, but I'm further from the oxygen leak.

event12.text: That's a trade I can live with. Literally.

event4.text: Of course that's the first thing he says.

event10.text: Operator, she once bypassed an alarm because it annoyed her.

event19.text: See? This is what he does.

event21.text: He makes you argue about the small thing so you stop looking at the blood on the floor.

event3.text: I know that probably felt wrong.

event5.text: He makes silence feel like betrayal.

event7.text: I don't have much time, so I'm going to be blunt.

event9.text: Hale is dead. I am not saying that because I heard a rumor.

event11.text: I saw what was left of the corridor.

event13.text: And now I need to get through the service spine before whatever is using his signal notices I'm moving.

event3.text: But if he starts steering this conversation, I cut the channel myself.

event5.text: And Ethan, before you correct my terminology, yes, I know it is technically a carrier interruption.

event7.text: I am calling it cutting the channel because it sounds better.

event9.text: I was not going to correct that.

event13.text: Operator, the longer both channels stay open, the uglier the carrier gets.

event15.text: Then help instead of narrating the problem.

event3.text: Left is the maintenance crawlway. Tight, probably safer, slower.

event5.text: Right is the main access spine. Faster, exposed, and I can see something moving in the reflection on the panel glass.

event7.text: I hate reflections. They always know things before I do.

event3.text: Great. I love survival choices that involve becoming luggage.

event10.text: There are scratch marks inside the paneling.

event14.text: I'm going to pretend I didn't see that until my body has time to schedule a reaction.

event3.text: Fast and stupid. Sometimes that works.

event13.text: I hit my shoulder on a hatch frame and said a word that would get me banned from two mission briefings.

event15.text: But I'm at Service Lock C.

event1.text: Service Lock C still has pressure.

event3.text: Barely. It keeps cycling like it can't decide whether to save me or make a point.

event5.text: There were six of us assigned to ECHO.

event7.text: Hale, Rourke, Lin, Patel, Arman... and me.

event11.text: Lin labeled everything, including things that were already labels.

event13.text: Patel pretended he hated everyone and fixed their coffee ratios when they weren't looking.

event15.text: Arman sang when he was nervous.

event18.text: He sang a lot near the end.

event20.text: See? That's the part records don't keep.

event22.text: They keep death times. Not people.

choice1.text: WHAT DO YOU NEED RIGHT NOW?

event1.text: You want proof. Good. Keep wanting proof.

event3.text: But understand this: the station is very good at giving you records after everyone who could explain them is gone.

event5.text: You will find logs. Timestamps. Clean little labels for ugly things.

event7.text: What you won't find is the sound Hale made when the corridor decompressed.

event12.text: If you need proof, get me to the service lock terminal. I can pull the survivor statement from my side.

event5.text: After that, water. After that, a room where nothing knows my name.

event7.text: Operationally: I need Service Lock C open without cycling the outer seal.

event9.text: Emotionally: ask me later. Preferably when my lungs stop voting against me.

event11.text: Get me to the terminal. I can send a statement and maybe keep myself alive.

event4.text: Screen is cracked. Buttons are sticky. I am choosing not to identify the substance.

event5.title: SURVIVOR STATEMENT: MAYA
event5.content:
TEXT TRANSCRIPT / CHANNEL 7.14

MAYA CHEN:
"There were six assigned crew members on ECHO. After the breach, only one confirmed biological survivor remained: me. The signal using Ethan Hale's identity is not a living crew member. Do not allow it to control station routing."

Transcript confidence: 91%
event5.caption: Text transcript reconstructed from Maya's stabilized emergency channel.

event9.text: That is such a clean phrase, isn't it?

event11.text: Like the station is politely avoiding the word alone.
